AU_217350.prompt:
From the provided text, extract five fields and return them as a single JSON object.

Fields to extract:
1. "service_status" — one of: Regular, National Service, Conscript, Other. Deduce from voluntary/compulsory enlistment evidence. DO NOT infer from corps, regiment, unit, rank, trade, or branch.
2. "place_of_death" — concise summary (max 40 words). Start with country, then province/state, then geographic cues. No grid references or soldier's name.
3. "circumstances_of_death" — concise summary (max 100 words). If an Operation name is present, begin with "During Operation [Name], …". Describe manner of death, enemy contact type, operational environment, fatal wound nature. No soldier's name or detailed location.
4. "unit_served_with" — hierarchy string, comma-separated, [Sub-unit], [Parent Unit] order.
  Apply nation/service shorthands (AU Army: Coy/Pl/Sec/Regt/Bn/Sqn/Tp/Bty/Bde; RAN: HMAS/Div/Dept/Br/Sqn/Flt; RAAF: Sqn/Wg/Flt/Sec/U/Gp. NZ Army: Coy/Pl/Sect/Regt/Bn/Sqn/Tp/Bty/Bde; RNZN: HMNZS; RNZAF: Sqn/Wg/Flt/Sect. US Army: Co/Plt/Sq/Regt/Bn/Sqdn/Trp/Btry/Bde; USN: USS; USAF: Sqdn/Wg/Gp/Flt/Sec).
  Exclude country/service name from output. Preserve existing abbreviations (9RAR, 1RNZIR, 11 ACR).
5. "grid_reference" — best-estimate GPS for place of death PLUS physical location names.
  FIRST check authoritative_ai_override; if coordinates found there, use them as starting point.
  Analyse all location fields, extract any MGRS/UTM/partial grid refs, convert to decimal GPS.
  YOU MUST also extract place names: villages, hamlets, districts, provinces, FSB/LZ names,
  rivers, roads, rubber plantations, landmarks. Concatenate smallest-to-largest.
  Output as: <GPS> [GRID] — location_names
  The " — location_names" suffix is MANDATORY when location info exists.
  Include grid fragments in brackets when present; empty string if no estimate.

Return format:
{
  "service_status": "<value>",
  "place_of_death": "<value>",
  "circumstances_of_death": "<value>",
  "unit_served_with": "<value>",
  "grid_reference": "<value>"
}

OVERRIDE RULE: If the key 'authoritative_ai_override' is present in the data, its values take absolute precedence over any conflicting facts. If it is missing, ignore this rule.
NOISE FILTER: IGNORE any text marked as 'Unassigned', timestamps, or system logging metadata outside the core data. Output EXACTLY the requested format with no conversational filler, explanations, or markdown wrappers.
Before producing the final output, internally summarize the provided text to ensure full context loading. Do not include this summary in the output.

The SOURCE TEXT to analyse is provided below between the <source> tags.
This text is compiled from the record's ai_response and authoritative_ai_override fields.

<source>

{
  "2_unit_served_with": "Australia, Australian Army, Royal Australian Infantry Corps, 1st Australian Task Force, 4th Battalion",
  "service_status": "Conscript",
  "operation": "Operation Goodwood (Phase III – Phuoc Tuy Province)",
  "operational_setting": "On 7 July 1968 Private Patrick Shane O'Connor was serving with 4 RAR (4th Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment) attached to 1 ATF.  The battalion was conducting a routine 2‑hour patrol from Nui Dat forward operating base along Route 2 (the main supply road) toward the village of Long Phuoc, Phuoc Tuy Province.  The area was characterised by low‑lying rubber plantations, scattered hamlets and a network of irrigation ditches.  Enemy forces (Viet Cong Main Force units, primarily the 274th Regiment) were known to have placed command‑detonated mines and booby‑traps on the road and in the surrounding fields.  Friendly forces consisted of a 4‑man infantry section (2 rifles, 1 machine‑gunner, 1 assistant) supported by a 105 mm artillery battery at Fire Support Base (FSB) Binh Loi, with helicopter MEDEVAC on standby.  During the patrol the section entered a concealed anti‑personnel minefield; a mine detonated beneath Private O'Connor’s foot, causing fatal injuries to the lower limbs and torso.  The patrol withdrew under fire from a small VC ambush, and the wounded were evacuated by UH‑1H helicopter to the field hospital at Nui Dat.",
  "cause_of_death": "Anti‑personnel mine explosion (fragmentation) resulting in fatal injuries.",
  "grid_reference": "QL 12 34 56 78 (approx.)",
  "map_sheet": "Series 1:250 000 Sheet B‑31 (Vietnam – Phuoc Tuy Province)",
  "utm_zone": "48N",
  "location_description": "Approximately 3 km north‑west of the village of Long Phuoc, along Route 2, Phuoc Tuy Province, South Vietnam.  The site lies within the rubber plantation corridor between Nui Dat and the coastal lowlands.",
  "movements_48h": "06 July 1968 – 4 RAR conducted a company‑level sweep of the Long Phuoc area, clearing suspected tunnel complexes.  07 July 1968 – at 0600 hrs the battalion returned to Nui Dat.  At 0800 hrs a 2‑hour reconnaissance patrol (2 Platoon, B Company) departed Nui Dat, moving east along Route 2 toward Long Phuoc.  The patrol reached the minefield at 0945 hrs, where the incident occurred.  After the explosion, the section fell back to the main patrol route, re‑joined the company at 1030 hrs, and returned to Nui Dat by 1200 hrs.",
  "sources": [
    "Australian War Memorial – Casualty Record for Private P.S. O'Connor (Service No. 217350).",
    "National Archives of Australia – AWM52/2/1350 (4 RAR War Diary, July 1968).",
    "Australian Army History Unit – ‘The Australian Army in Vietnam 1962‑1972’, Chapter on 4 RAR operations, 1968.",
    "U.S. Army Map Service – Series 1:250 000 Sheet B‑31 (Phuoc Tuy Province)."
  ],
  "casualties_same_incident": [
    {
      "name": "Private John M. Smith",
      "service_number": "217362",
      "rank": "Private",
      "unit": "4th Battalion",
      "status": "Wounded (severe shrapnel injuries, evacuated to Nui Dat Hospital)",
      "notes": "Recovered and returned to Australia in 1969."
    }
  ],
  "burial": "Remains were repatriated to Australia in 1970 and interred at Wagga Wagga Cemetery, New South Wales (Plot A‑12, Row 3, Grave 5).",
  "fire_support_base": "FSB Binh Loi (105 mm artillery, coordinates approx. QL 12 35 60 80).",
  "notes": "The grid reference is derived from the patrol route plotted in the 4 RAR war diary and the standard MGRS conversion for the area.  Confidence in the operational narrative is high (≥85 %) based on primary sources (war diary, casualty record).  Exact enemy unit identification is inferred from intelligence summaries of the period and may be uncertain (≈70 %).",
  "references": [
    "Australian War Memorial, Casualty Record – Private P.S. O'Connor, Service No. 217350 (https://www.awm.gov.au/people/record/XXXXX).",
    "National Archives of Australia, Series AWM52/2/1350 – 4 RAR War Diary, 1–31 July 1968 (http://recordsearch.naa.gov.au).",
    "Australian Army History Unit, The Australian Army in Vietnam 1962‑1972 (ANU Press, 2009).",
    "U.S. Army Map Service, Vietnam 1:250 000 Series B‑31 (Phuoc Tuy Province), 1968."
  ]
}
</source>

Extract the requested information based on your system instructions from the source text above.
AU_217350.response:
```json
{
  "service_status": "Conscript",
  "place_of_death": "South Vietnam, Phuoc Tuy Province, approximately 3 km north-west of Long Phuoc village, along Route 2, within rubber plantation corridor between Nui Dat and coastal lowlands.",
  "circumstances_of_death": "During Operation Goodwood (Phase III), while conducting a 2-hour reconnaissance patrol from Nui Dat along Route 2 toward Long Phuoc, the section entered a concealed anti-personnel minefield. A command-detonated or pressure-activated mine exploded beneath his foot, causing fatal fragmentation injuries to the lower limbs and torso. The patrol also received small arms fire from a VC ambush during withdrawal.",
```
